# A top-kernel teardown report generated by nvidia-kaggle

- 投稿者: Jiwei Liu
- 投稿日時: 2026-07-03 22:04:43.202000
- 投票数: 25
- コメント数: 2（取得数: 2）
- トピックID: `718670`
- 原文: [https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction/discussion/718670](https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction/discussion/718670)

## 本文

<p>One pain point of kaggle agents is automatically tracking the kernels with correct versions. We recently open sourced <a href="https://github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-kaggle">https://github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-kaggle</a> The writeup below is generated by the following prompt in claude code. </p>
<p>Please star our repo if you find it useful 😄</p>
<blockquote>
  <p>use /nvidia-kaggle agent skill to write a markdown report that can be drop in pasted as a kaggle post discussion. analyze top scored kernels of <a href="https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction">https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction</a> and write a top kernel report for each selected kernel, show the score of each version. is it possible to check if there's any diff between versions? sometimes different versions are identical code rerun. also show kernel dependencies.</p>
</blockquote>
<p><em>Compiled 2026-07-03 with the <a href="https://github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-kaggle">nvidia-kaggle skill</a>. Every public-LB number below was fetched <strong>per version</strong> from Kaggle's kernel-version API; every "identical / changed" label comes from a <strong>byte-level diff of the two versions' code</strong>, not from the notebook title. Metric is <strong>RMSE, lower is better</strong>. For context, the public leaderboard leader on this date sits at <strong>5.262</strong> — well under any public notebook — so treat public-notebook scores as a floor to clear, not the ceiling.</em></p>
<p><a href="https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction">Competition page</a></p>
<hr>
<h2>Why this post exists</h2>
<p>Kaggle stores <strong>every</strong> version of a public notebook with its leaderboard score, and in the browser you <em>can</em> click into a version and see a diff — fine for a human eyeballing one notebook. But doing that <strong>programmatically, across many kernels</strong> — pulling each version's code, diffing it, and deciding whether a score move was a real edit or a <strong>lucky rerun of identical code</strong> — is exactly what a human clicking around can't scale to and what an LLM agent has no direct API for. That's the gap the nvidia-kaggle agent skill closes, and it's the machinery behind this whole post. It matters a lot here, because the strongest ROGII notebooks are <strong>stochastic</strong> (particle filters, random seeds), so the same code can land anywhere in a ~0.4-RMSE band from run to run — and eyeballing one diff won't tell you that.</p>
<p>This teardown answers three questions for eight of the most-upvoted public kernels:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>What did each version actually score?</strong> (verified, per version)</li>
<li><strong>Was the change real, or an identical-code rerun?</strong> (byte-level code diff)</li>
<li><strong>What does each kernel depend on, and at which version?</strong> (data/kernel sources)</li>
</ol>
<h3>How the "identical vs changed" call is made</h3>
<p><img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daxiongshu/competition-brief-demo/main/claude_rogii_top_kernel_report_004/plots/diff_method.png" alt="How a real edit is told apart from an identical rerun"></p>
<p>For each version I download the exact source (<code>kernel_archive.py --version N</code>), strip cell outputs and execution counts, normalize trailing whitespace and blank lines, then hash and unified-diff consecutive versions. <strong>Same hash ⇒ identical rerun</strong> (any score move is pure run-to-run noise); <strong>non-empty diff ⇒ real change</strong> (I then read the actual edited lines). <em>SVG source: <code>plots/diff_method.svg</code>.</em></p>
<hr>
<h2>The eight kernels at a glance</h2>
<p>Sorted by community votes. "Best LB" is the lowest verified public RMSE across the kernel's history; "best ver" is which version produced it — <strong>often not the latest</strong>.</p>

<p><strong>Headline:</strong> for three of these kernels the <em>best</em> score sits on a version whose code is <strong>identical</strong> to a version that scored differently — i.e. the "best" is partly luck. Read on.</p>
<hr>
<h2>Per-version scores + diff verdicts</h2>
<p>Below, <strong>∆code</strong> is added/removed lines vs the previous version (from the byte-diff); "identical" means the normalized code hash matched the previous version exactly.</p>
<h3>1. nihilisticneuralnet — DWT-based (645 votes) — <em>the "same code, four scores" case</em></h3>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Ver</th>
<th>Public LB</th>
<th>vs prev code</th>
<th>Note</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1</td>
<td>9.631</td>
<td>—</td>
<td>title <em>"Hill climbing 17bb04"</em></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2</td>
<td>9.632</td>
<td><strong>identical</strong> (+0/−0)</td>
<td>rerun</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3</td>
<td><strong>9.251</strong></td>
<td><strong>identical</strong> (+0/−0)</td>
<td>rerun — this is the number in the title</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4</td>
<td>9.395</td>
<td><strong>identical</strong> (+0/−0)</td>
<td>rerun</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>All four versions share one code hash (<code>c337477a</code>). The notebook is titled after its best rerun (9.251), but <strong>the code that produced 9.632 and 9.395 is the same code</strong>. This is the clearest example in the competition that a headline LB in a title can be a <em>sample from a noisy distribution</em>, not a reproducible result.</p>
<h3>2. cdeotte — XGB Starter (190 votes) — <em>the highest-leverage 4 lines</em></h3>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Ver</th>
<th>Public LB</th>
<th>vs prev code</th>
<th>The actual edit</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1</td>
<td>19.047</td>
<td>—</td>
<td>residual model over a <em>recent-slope</em> baseline</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2</td>
<td><em>(no score — run did not submit)</em></td>
<td>—</td>
<td></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3</td>
<td><strong>14.336</strong></td>
<td>changed (+4/−4)</td>
<td>switched the residual baseline to <strong>flat last-known-TVT</strong>; keeps slope baselines only as features</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>A four-line change — <code>cur["baseline_tvt"] = last_known_tvt</code> instead of <code>..._recent_slope</code>, plus dropping a test-row-map arg — cut <strong>4.7 RMSE</strong>. This is the reference "anchor on the last known <code>TVT_input</code>, predict the residual" trick, and its diff is the cheapest big win in the whole set.</p>
<h3>3. ravaghi — "Ridge" (260 votes) — <em>a textbook hyperparameter grind</em></h3>
<p>Despite the name, this kernel is a <strong>particle-filter ensemble blended with Ridge</strong>. Every version is a 1–2 line tweak; nothing is a rerun.</p>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Ver</th>
<th>Public LB</th>
<th>vs prev code</th>
<th>The actual edit</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1</td>
<td>8.129</td>
<td>—</td>
<td>PF ensemble, blend <code>0.3·tvt₁ + 0.7·tvt₂</code></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2</td>
<td>8.308</td>
<td>changed (+1/−1)</td>
<td>blend → <code>0.25 / 0.75</code> <em>(worse)</em></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>3</td>
<td>8.290</td>
<td>changed (+1/−1)</td>
<td>blend → <code>0.35 / 0.65</code></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>4</td>
<td>8.252</td>
<td>changed (+2/−2)</td>
<td>PF <code>n_seeds 128→256</code>; blend → <code>0.3 / 0.7</code></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>5</td>
<td>8.125</td>
<td>changed (+1/−1)</td>
<td>PF <code>n_particles 500→600</code></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>6</td>
<td><strong>7.946</strong></td>
<td>changed (+1/−1)</td>
<td>PF <code>n_seeds 256→150</code> <em>(the winning move)</em></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>The trajectory wobbles (v2 got worse) but trends down. The final gain came from <em>fewer</em> seeds (150) with <em>more</em> particles (600) — worth knowing before you copy someone's seed count as gospel.</p>
<h3>4. pixiux — Dual-Pipeline Blend (229 votes) & 5. lightningv08 — Self-Verifying (281 votes) — <em>a cross-author fork</em></h3>
<table>

<th>Kernel · Ver</th>
<th>Public LB</th>
<th>vs prev code</th>
<th>Note</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>pixiux v1</td>
<td><strong>7.519</strong></td>
<td>—</td>
<td><code>.py</code> script; best of the three</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>pixiux v2</td>
<td>7.560</td>
<td>changed (+43/−0)</td>
<td><code>.py</code>, added logic</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>pixiux v3</td>
<td>7.559</td>
<td>changed (+62/−88)</td>
<td>rewritten as a <strong>notebook</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>lightningv08 v1</td>
<td>7.470</td>
<td><strong>identical to pixiux v3</strong></td>
<td>forked, ran, scored 0.089 better <em>for free</em></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>lightningv08 v2</td>
<td>7.559</td>
<td>changed (+101/−33)</td>
<td>added a <strong>self-verifying</strong> 2-submission blend selector</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p><code>lightningv08/…-self-verifying</code> <strong>version 1 is byte-for-byte identical</strong> to <code>pixiux/…-blend</code> version 3 (verified: both 110,785 bytes, SHA <code>c952972e</code>). Same code, different public score (7.470 vs 7.559) — the stochastic band again. lightningv08's <em>own</em> contribution lands in v2 (a self-verifying blend of the fleongg + SP45 submissions), which traded 0.09 of public LB for robustness.</p>
<h3>6. bernubritz — LB7295 Public Rebuild (442 votes)</h3>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Ver</th>
<th>Public LB</th>
<th>vs prev code</th>
<th>Note</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>1</td>
<td><strong>7.153</strong></td>
<td>—</td>
<td>best version</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>2</td>
<td><em>(no score)</em></td>
<td>—</td>
<td>run did not submit</td>
</tr>
<tr>

<td>7.302</td>
<td><strong>identical to v1</strong> (+0/−0)</td>
<td>rerun scored 0.149 worse</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p>The 2nd-most-upvoted kernel, and its best score is v1 — a later <strong>identical rerun</strong> did <em>worse</em>. If you fork this, fork <strong>v1</strong>.</p>
<h3>7 & 8. The long grinds — real iteration, but read the noise</h3>
<p>These two have too many versions to tabulate line-by-line, but the shape is the story (see the trajectory panel below). Verified best scores:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>pilkwang — Target-Free TVT Geosteering: 42 versions</strong>, 8.004 (v1) → <strong>7.501 (v36)</strong>, then drifts back up to ~7.58. Genuine iteration — but the last version is <strong>not</strong> the best; v36 is.</li>
<li><strong>degnonguidi — Public Score [LB 7.159]: 18 versions</strong>, best <strong>7.159 (v16)</strong>. History is noisy: several versions failed to score, and <strong>v9 scored 3459.018</strong> (a broken submission). Best ≠ latest here either.</li>
</ul>
<hr>
<h2>Seeing it: version score trajectories</h2>
<p><img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daxiongshu/competition-brief-demo/main/claude_rogii_top_kernel_report_004/plots/version_trajectories.png" alt="Per-version public LB for the six fully-diffed kernels"></p>
<p><strong>Takeaway:</strong> the green ring (best version) lands on the <em>latest</em> version only twice (ravaghi, cdeotte). For DWT, pixiux and bernubritz the best score is an earlier version — and for DWT/bernubritz it sits on <strong>code identical</strong> to a worse-scoring sibling. If you're forking for a submission, pick the version by <em>verified score</em>, not by "latest".</p>
<h2>The core finding: same code, different leaderboard score</h2>
<p><img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daxiongshu/competition-brief-demo/main/claude_rogii_top_kernel_report_004/plots/rerun_noise.png" alt="Identical-code groups and the LB spread they produce"></p>
<p>Three independent cases where <strong>byte-identical code</strong> produced <strong>different public LB scores</strong>:</p>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Identical-code group</th>
<th>Score spread (RMSE)</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>DWT v1–v4 (one hash)</td>
<td>9.251 → 9.632 = <strong>0.381</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>LB7295 Rebuild v1 & v3</td>
<td>7.153 → 7.302 = <strong>0.149</strong></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>pixiux v3 ≡ lightningv08 v1 (fork)</td>
<td>7.470 → 7.559 = <strong>0.089</strong></td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p><strong>Implication for your own work:</strong> a 0.1–0.4 RMSE move on the public LB between two of your submissions can be <em>pure rerun variance</em>. Don't trust a single run of a stochastic pipeline; average seeds and judge on CV. And treat "LB x.xxx" in a notebook title as <em>a draw from a distribution</em>, not a guarantee.</p>
<h2>Does the size of a code change predict the score change? No.</h2>
<p><img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daxiongshu/competition-brief-demo/main/claude_rogii_top_kernel_report_004/plots/change_vs_delta.png" alt="Lines of code touched vs change in public LB"></p>
<p>Two extremes make the point: <strong>cdeotte moved 4 lines and gained 4.7 RMSE</strong>; <strong>pixiux rewrote ~150 lines (script → notebook) and moved essentially 0</strong>. Meanwhile the identical reruns (0 lines) still drift ±0.4. Read the <em>diff</em>, not the line count.</p>
<hr>
<h2>Dependencies (with version info)</h2>
<p><img src="https://raw.githubusercontent.com/daxiongshu/competition-brief-demo/main/claude_rogii_top_kernel_report_004/plots/dependency_web.png" alt="Featured-kernel dependency web"></p>
<p><em>SVG source: <code>plots/dependency_web.svg</code>.</em> Kaggle attaches data/kernel sources <strong>unpinned</strong> — a notebook references a dataset by <code>owner/slug</code> and resolves to that dataset's <strong>latest</strong> version at run time. So the versions below are each dataset's <strong>current</strong> version (fetched 2026-07-03), which is what a fork of these kernels loads today.</p>
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Shared data source</th>
<th>Cur. version</th>
<th>Size</th>
<th>Used by (of the 8)</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td><code>ravaghi/wellbore-geology-prediction-artifacts</code></td>
<td><strong>v6</strong></td>
<td>2.36 GB</td>
<td><strong>7</strong> — dwt, ridge, pixiux, lightningv08, bernubritz, pilkwang, degnonguidi</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>fleongg/rogii-claude-models-pub</code></td>
<td>v1</td>
<td>0.01 GB</td>
<td>5 — pixiux, lightningv08, bernubritz, pilkwang, degnonguidi</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>phongnguyn23021656/koolbox-offline</code></td>
<td>v1</td>
<td>0.05 GB</td>
<td>4 — pixiux, lightningv08, bernubritz, degnonguidi <em>(offline pip wheels — internet is off in this comp)</em></td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>pilkwang/rogii-model-package</code></td>
<td>v9</td>
<td>0.22 GB</td>
<td>1 — pilkwang</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>thbdh5765/rogii-v10-fresh-artifacts</code></td>
<td>v1</td>
<td>0.41 GB</td>
<td>1 — degnonguidi</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>needless090/rogii-tabicl-mirror</code></td>
<td>v1</td>
<td>0.11 GB</td>
<td>1 — degnonguidi</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td><code>nina2025/rogii-03</code></td>
<td>v5</td>
<td><0.01 GB</td>
<td>1 — degnonguidi</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
<p><strong>Two things worth knowing:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>One dataset is the backbone.</strong> <code>ravaghi/…-artifacts</code> (v6, 2.36 GB — it ships <code>data/train.csv</code> plus pre-trained CatBoost models) is loaded by <strong>7 of the 8</strong> kernels. If it's ever revised, a lot of public notebooks shift underneath their authors. The blend family additionally shares the exact same trio (<code>koolbox-offline</code> + <code>rogii-claude-models-pub</code> + <code>artifacts</code>), consistent with them being forks of one lineage.</li>
<li><strong><code>kernel_sources</code> are mostly noise.</strong> Every kernel's <code>kernel_sources</code> field points at auto-generated <code>packagemanager/pm-…</code> entries (Kaggle's offline-package snapshots), <strong>not</strong> at another notebook — so the metadata does <em>not</em> reveal the pixiux → lightningv08 fork. The only way I found that lineage was the <strong>byte-identical code hash</strong>. Metadata under-reports real code reuse in this competition; diff the source.</li>
</ul>
<hr>
<h2>Practical takeaways</h2>
<ol>
<li><strong>Pick a fork version by verified score, not "latest."</strong> For DWT, pixiux, and bernubritz the best score is an earlier version; for DWT/bernubritz that best is a <em>lucky rerun</em> of code that also scored worse.</li>
<li><strong>Expect ±0.1–0.4 RMSE of rerun noise</strong> on the stochastic pipelines. Average seeds; trust CV over a single public-LB draw.</li>
<li><strong>The cheapest verified win is cdeotte's residual-baseline swap</strong> (recent-slope → last-known-TVT): 4 lines, −4.7 RMSE. Start there.</li>
<li><strong>The public frontier here (~7.15–7.5) is a shared-lineage plateau</strong> built on one artifacts dataset + a couple of model packages. Real separation on the private LB (leader 5.26) will come from <em>decorrelating</em> from that lineage, not re-forking it.</li>
<li><strong>Don't trust <code>kernel_sources</code> for lineage</strong> — diff the code hashes.</li>
</ol>
<h2>Reproduce this</h2>
<pre><code># per-version public LB scores for any kernel
python ./scripts/kernel_archive.py <owner/slug> --scores-only

# download a specific version's source to diff
python ./scripts/kernel_archive.py <owner/slug> <out_dir> --version N

# dependency sources
kaggle kernels pull <owner/slug> -m   # then read kernel-metadata.json
</code></pre>
<p>The diff classifier (<code>work/diff_versions.py</code>), the consolidated data (<code>work/data.json</code>), and the figure/SVG generators (<code>plots/make_plots.py</code>, <code>plots/make_svgs.py</code>) are all in this folder — every number above traces back to them.</p>
<hr>
<h3>Kernels referenced</h3>

### コメント 2 — Georgy Mamarin

- 投稿日時: 2026-07-05 17:38:42.223000
- 投票数: -2
- コメントID: `3489522`

<p>Same wall from the tooling side — and it lines up with what I measured from the estimation side. Your three identical-code spreads (0.38 / 0.15 / 0.09) are the seed band made visible by byte-diffing the version history; pilkwang put a hard number on the same effect in one of my threads — byte-identical notebooks spanning 7.168–7.286, ~0.12 from nothing but the reseed. So a fork reading 0.03 better than its neighbour is almost always a draw from that band.</p>
<p>The "decorrelate, don't re-fork" line is the part I'd underline, and there's a measurable reason the ~7.2 plateau is sticky: the two strongest public engines carry error-correlation ρ ≈ 0.89 (fle3n's blend diagnosis), so a convex re-weight recovers under 0.001 RMSE — a correlation ceiling, not a tuning gap. New signal has to come from outside that lineage — which is your dependency-web point (one artifacts dataset under 7 of 8), reached from the other side.</p>
